coredata.dc:description: Non-specific binding in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) remains a challenge in foodborne pathogen detection, resulting in interference of high background signals. Herein, we innovatively reported a dual-mode FRET sensor based on a “noise purifier” for the ultrasensitive quantification of Escherichia coli O157:H7 in food. An efficient FRET system was constructed with polymyxin B-modified nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs@PMB) as donors and aptamer-modified yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs@Apt) as acceptors. Magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Fe@MWCNTs) were employed as a “noise purifier” to reduce the interference of the fluorescence background. Under the background purification mode, the sensitivity of the dual-mode signals of the FRET sensor has increased by an order of magnitude. Additionally, smartphone-assisted colorimetric analysis enabled point-of-care detection of E. coli O157:H7 in real samples. The developed sensing platform based on a “noise purifier” provides a promising method for ultrasensitive on-site testing of trace pathogenic bacteria in various foodstuffs.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive smartphone-assisted bicolor-ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on a “noise purifier” for point-of-care testing of pathogenic bacteria in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-specific binding in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) remains a challenge in foodborne pathogen detection, resulting in interference of high background signals. Herein, we innovatively reported a dual-mode FRET sensor based on a “noise purifier” for the ultrasensitive quantification of Escherichia coli O157:H7 in food. An efficient FRET system was constructed with polymyxin B-modified nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs@PMB) as donors and aptamer-modified yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs@Apt) as acceptors. Magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Fe@MWCNTs) were employed as a “noise purifier” to reduce the interference of the fluorescence background. Under the background purification mode, the sensitivity of the dual-mode signals of the FRET sensor has increased by an order of magnitude. Additionally, smartphone-assisted colorimetric analysis enabled point-of-care detection of E. coli O157:H7 in real samples. The developed sensing platform based on a “noise purifier” provides a promising method for ultrasensitive on-site testing of trace pathogenic bacteria in various foodstuffs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) is a common foodborne pathogen. Symptoms and diseases caused by E. coli infection include abdominal cramps, watery diarrhea, hemolytic uremic syndrome, and ulcerative colitis, which can be fatal in severe cases (Bai et al., 2022). In addition, even low levels of infection can pose a high public health risk (Lou, Jia, Rong, Zhang, Zhang, & Du, 2022). Therefore, “zero tolerance” detection of E. coli O157:H7 is necessary. To date, a number of techniques have been proposed to identify E. coli O157:H7, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Shrestha & Dorevitch, 2019), nucleic acid isothermal amplification (Lee & Oh, 2022), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Wang et al., 2019), among others. However, the application of these techniques is severely hampered by the disadvantages of cumbersome operation, high false-positive rate, poor stability, high cost, and low sensitivity. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop new techniques that meet the comprehensive performance requirements for rapidity, sensitivity, specificity, and economy.

Point-of-care testing (POCT) based on smartphone analytics has emerged as one of the most promising on-site detection platforms, given its advantages of portability, real-time, and easy operation. Combining it with fluorescence imaging can further enable highly sensitive detection of E. coli in food matrices. Hiremath et al. designed a POCT platform based on carbon dots (CDs)-manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets, which presented a concentration-dependent fluorescence signal response to live E. coli through the FRET effect with a detection limit of 50 cfu/mL (Hiremath et al., 2021). You et al. reported a monochromatic fluorescence sensor based on phage@DNAzyme probe, combined with RGB analysis of fluorescence color changes by a smartphone, which enabled quantitative detection of E. coli O157:H7 in the range of 102 ∼ 108 cfu/mL (You et al., 2023). However, the above-mentioned fluorescence sensors mainly rely on single-wavelength fluorescence emission, which is susceptible to interference from non-target substances in food matrices, reducing the detection accuracy and the dynamic response range of food contaminants. In terms of reducing the influence of external factors and establishing an effective contamination early warning mechanism, the responsive ratiometric fluorescence analysis platform shows excellent application prospects (Y. Shen, Wei, Zhu, Cao, & Han, 2022).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a common mechanism in ratiometric fluorescence assays (Zhang, Liu, Yang, Li, Tao, & Song, 2023). The FRET mechanism refers to the fact that when two fluorescent chromophores are sufficiently close (<10 nm), energy can be transferred from the donor to the acceptor, causing a shift in the fluorescence intensity of the double emission peak, thereby achieving a dynamic response to pathogenic bacteria (Y. Shen et al., 2022). Unfortunately, even in the absence of target bacteria in the sample matrix, unexpected binding between donors and acceptors may occur due to non-covalent interaction forces, such as electrostatic attraction (He et al., 2023), which can result in the negative effect of a high background signal for detection. To improve the detection signal-to-noise ratio of FRET sensors, efficient removal of acceptors that do not specifically bind to the target bacteria is critical. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were capable of adsorbing large amounts of nucleic acid aptamers based on non-covalent interactions (Kannappan et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2003). However, MWCNTs-based nanoplatforms required multiple high-speed centrifugations for target detection, and the operation steps were cumbersome (Barbosa et al., 2019). The synthesized magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Fe@MWCNTs) could be efficiently separated under magnetic force by doping carbonyl iron powder (CIP) to endow MWCNTs with magnetic properties, which could be used as a tunable signal switch to realize the “adsorption-desorption” flexible switching of fluorescent acceptors (Y-CDs@Apt). So far, no relevant reports have been found on any ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on Fe@MWCNTs to get rid of background interference for pathogenic bacteria detection.

The selection of fluorescent nanomaterials is of great significance for constructing FRET sensors. The efficiency of FRET is positively correlated with the fluorescence emission efficiency of the donor, and the donor with high luminescence can improve the detection sensitivity. Doping N and S elements into graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) increases their fluorescence quantum yield. Cross-linking graphene cores with l-cysteine prevents aggregation between nanoparticles (Xia, Hai, Chen, & Wang, 2017). These characteristics make N, S-GQDs an excellent donor material. Screening tests revealed that the emission spectra of N, S-GQDs highly overlapped with the absorption spectra of yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs). In addition, Y-CDs have a positive surface potential derived from nitrogen-containing organic precursors, and the abundant amino groups on their surfaces not only enhance their fluorescence but also provide covalent binding sites for carboxylated aptamer probes (Yao, Liu, Zhao, Liu, Fang, & Wang, 2021). Therefore, Y-CDs were selected as acceptors to form an efficient FRET detection system with N, S-GQDs.

The primary purpose of molecular recognition elements is to provide analyte specificity for biosensors. Currently, most ratiometric fluorescence detection strategies utilize vancomycin as a specific recognition element for Gram-positive bacteria (Hu, Pang, Biswas, Wang, Wang, & Xia, 2021; W. Li et al., 2023). In contrast, ratiometric detection methods using recognition elements of negative bacteria have rarely been reported. Polymyxin B (PMB) is an antibiotic derived from Bacillus polymyxa. It is a cationic cyclic polypeptide that effectively targets the lipopolysaccharides found in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria at low concentrations (M. Zhao et al., 2023). Furthermore, combined with the high selectivity of the aptamer (Apt), broad-spectrum and highly specific recognition of E. coli O157:H7 can be achieved. Based on the above dual-recognition scheme and the excellent characteristics of fluorescent nanomaterials, combined with the background reduction effect of the “purifier”, a novel idea for ultrasensitive and accurate detection of pathogenic bacteria in the field has been generated.

In this study, we constructed for the first time a bicolor-ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on background elimination using a “purifier” for the ultrasensitive and accurate detection of E. coli O157:H7 (Scheme 1 ). The sensors used N, S-GQDs@PMB (PMB-modified N, S-GQDs) as energy donors and Y-CDs@Apt (Apt-modified Y-CDs) as energy acceptors. When E. coli O157:H7 was introduced, the donor and acceptor specifically adsorbed onto the surface of the target bacteria, shortening the distance between them, and FRET was turned on, resulting in a reversible change in the dual fluorescence signal. On the other hand, the excess Y-CDs@Apt in the system, which were not bound to the target bacteria, were adsorbed on the “noise purifier” due to π-π stacking and efficiently separated by magnetic separation, thus reducing the fluorescence interference. Further, the ratiometric fluorescence modality detection platform assisted by the “noise purifier” showed significant concentration-dependent fluorescence color changes. By integrating with a portable smartphone detection platform, visual fluorescence images dependent on E. coli O157:H7 concentration can be captured under the excitation of a built-in UV lamp, enabling on-site colorimetric modal analysis. Notably, this on-site detection strategy shows good feasibility and high sensitivity in practical food samples, which is revolutionary for the development of built-in self-calibrating biosensors for E. coli O157:H7 trace detection.

E. coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43895), E. coli (CMCC 44829, ATCC 8739, CMCC 44151, CMCC 44102), Shigella flexneri (ATCC 12022), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 27853), Vibrio parahaemolyticus (ATCC 17802), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 19115), Pseudomonas putida (ATCC 49128), Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 14028), Enterococcus faecalis (ATCC 29212), and Cronobacter sakazakii (ATCC 29544) were obtained from the Guangdong Institute of Microbiology (Guangdong, China). Vibrio parahaemolyticus was inoculated into Luria-Bertani medium (LB) containing 3 % sodium chloride, and other pathogenic bacteria were inoculated in LB. The culture was incubated at 150 rpm and 37 °C by shaking for 12 h, and the bacterial solution was collected when the bacteria were cultured to the logarithmic growth phase. Before use, broth cultures were centrifuged at 4 °C, 5000g for 5 min in sterile centrifuge tubes and resuspended in PBS buffer (PBS = 0.1 M, pH = 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Y-CDs and N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A one-pot hydrothermal method was used to prepare Y-CDs. First, 216 mg of o-phenylenediamine was added to 30 mL of deionized water and mixed well by shaking. The mixture was subsequently transferred to a sealed hydrothermal reaction vessel and subjected to a reaction at 160 °C for 4 h, then gradually cooled to room temperature. The reaction products were centrifuged at 9289g for 12 min to eliminate impurities. Then, they were placed into a dialysis bag with a permeability of 3000 Da and dialyzed with deionized water for 24 h to obtain the dialysate. Finally, the dialysate was dried in a vacuum freeze dryer for 12 h to obtain solid Y-CDs. The synthesis of N, S-GQDs was slightly modified from the previous study (Qiu, Na, Li, Bai, Zhang, & Jin, 2022). Briefly, 0.3 g of l-cysteine and 1.0 g of citric acid were evenly mixed in a beaker and then heated to 200 °C in an oil bath. Until the color of the reactants changed from light yellow to orange, the reaction was terminated and naturally cooled to room temperature. The orange product was then dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water to gain the N, S-GQDs stock solution. Finally, the N, S-GQDs stock solution was dried in a vacuum freeze dryer for 12 h to get N, S-GQDs solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Y-CDs@Apt and N, S-GQDs@PMB
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Y-CDs@Apt included the following steps: First, 100 μL of the mixture containing 2 mg/mL EDC and 2 mg/mL NHS was added to 300 μL of anti-E. coli O157:H7 aptamer solution (1 μM), and incubated at 180 rpm for 30 min under ambient temperature to obtain the activated aptamer solution. Then, 5 mg of Y-CDs solid was dissolved into 5 mL of deionized water, to which the activated aptamer solution was added, and incubated at 180 rpm for 2.5 h at room temperature to obtain Y-CDs@Apt. The N, S-GQDs and PMB were joined through the amidation reaction of carboxyl groups with amino groups. First, 10 mg of N, S-GQDs solid was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 10 min. Then, 5 mg of NHS and 6 mg of EDC were added. The mixture was stirred for 2 h at room temperature to activate the –COOH on the surface of the N, S-GQDs. Finally, 44 mg of PMB was added and stirred at 200 rpm for 7 h at room temperature to obtain N, S-GQDs@PMB.

A one-step method was used to prepare Fe@MWCNTs. First, 1.5 mg of MWCNTs was weighed into a glass vial, and 1.5 mL of MES solution with a concentration of 0.1 M and pH 5.2 was added and dissolved by sufficient shaking. Then 200 μL of a mixed solution of EDC (40 mM) and NHS (40 mM) was added and activated for 2 h at room temperature. Finally, 3 mg of CIP was added and sonicated for 45 min, then dried in an oven at 85 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E. coli O157:H7 by detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1D illustrates the procedure of the “noise purifier” -ratiometric fluorescence biosensor (NP-RF biosensor). First, 400 μL of E. coli O157:H7 at different concentrations (0, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108 cfu/mL) was taken into a sterilized centrifuge tube, to which 60 μL of Y-CDs@Apt-Fe@MWCNTs (prepared by incubating 60 μL of Y-CDs@Apt and 140 μg of Fe@MWCNTs at 25 °C for 20 min) were added, and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min to obtain a composite solution. The composite solution was then placed on a magnetic separation rack and after resting for 1 min, the supernatant was collected. Finally, 40 μL of N, S-GQDs@PMB was added to the supernatant and incubated at 37 °C for 25 min to obtain the solution to be measured. The solution to be measured was transferred to a cuvette, and the fluorescence intensity values of the solution to be tested at 422 nm and 566 nm were read under the excitation spectrum at 355 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio F566 nm/F422 nm was used to calculate the concentration of E. coli O157:H7 in the solution to be measured. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the following formula: LOD = 3σ/slope (σ: standard deviation of 11 measurements of the blank sample) (Lou et al., 2022). The ratiometric fluorescence biosensor (RF biosensor) without the “noise purifier” was performed in the same manner as the NP-RF biosensor, except that Fe@MWCNTs were not added.

To achieve field detection, we designed a portable smartphone-integrated device. The device consisted of a smartphone, mobile power supply, carrier, 365 nm UV lamp, 400 nm filter, sample stage, and cellulose filter paper (Fig. S1). The 365 nm UV lamp equipped with a mobile power supply was immobilized in the carrier as an excitation light source. The cellulose filter paper was placed on the sample stage as the imaging carrier. The 400 nm filter was placed between the sample stage and the smartphone. The entire test environment was completely dark, which eliminated interference from the external environment. In addition, the distance between the smartphone camera and the test sample was fixed, which ensured the reliability and repeatability of the experiment. The operational steps for the on-site detection method based on smartphone devices (“Noise Purifier”-based on-site biosensor, NP-on-site biosensor; on-site biosensor) are as follows. First, 50 μL of NP-RF biosensor or RF biosensor “the solution to be measured” was dropped onto a cellulose filter paper (2 cm × 1 cm). After drying naturally for 60 s, the cellulose filter paper was placed in the sample stage. Subsequently, the built-in 365 nm UV lamp was turned on and the fluorescence image was captured by the smartphone camera after passing through the filter (400 nm). The installed Color Picker APP converted the collected fluorescence images into signal values representing three color channels: red (R), green (G), and blue (B). Since the ratio of the red channel to the blue channel was linearly correlated with the concentration of E. coli O157:H7, the detection results were output as R/B values. Each sample was tested by the Color Picker APP three times, and the average R/B value was taken to ensure the representativeness of the experimental data. Each experiment was repeated in triplicate.

To further verify the applicability of the proposed method in detecting real samples. Milk, drinking water, and beef purchased from a local supermarket (Hefei, China) were spiked with 101, 103, and 105 cfu/mL of E. coli O157:H7, respectively. No pre-treatment was required after adding the target bacteria to the drinking water. The beef was pre-treated according to the reported method (Chen et al., 2023). First, 25 g of beef samples were weighed, minced, and placed in a homogenizer for 2 min. Then, 25 mL of PBS buffer containing 3 % trichloroacetic acid was added and shaken for 10 min. Finally, the samples were centrifuged, and the supernatant obtained was used for analysis. The pre-treatment of milk samples mainly consisted of the following steps: 1 mL milk sample was centrifuged at 5000g for 5 min, and the fat-containing supernatant was discarded, washed three times, and resuspended in an equal volume of PBS solution. Once the samples had been processed, the test was carried out according to the procedure described above (Section 2.5). The specificity test was repeated in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, S-GQDs@PMB and Y-CDs@Apt
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with the single-emission fluorescence method, the ratiometric fluorescence method has a built-in self-calibration function that effectively avoids the output of false positive results. The key to successfully constructing a ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on the FRET mechanism lies in selecting donors and acceptors (Lai et al., 2023; X. Shen, Xu, Ouyang, & Na, 2022). A one-pot hydrothermal method was used to synthesize N, S-GQDs. EDS elemental analysis revealed successful doping of GQDs with N and S elements (Fig. S2A-E). The doping of heteroatoms in GQDs contributes to the uniformity of the surface state and effectively increases their fluorescence quantum yield (Shin, Guo, Zhao, & Guo, 2019). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the prepared N, S-GQDs were spherical and well dispersed, with an average size of about 2.9 nm (inset of Fig. 1 B). The synthesis of N, S-GQDs@PMB was achieved by activating the carboxyl group of N, S-GQDs through NHS/EDC and combining with amino-rich PMB through amide reaction (Fig. 1A). The center diameter of the prepared N, S-GQDs was found to be 9.8 nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. After loading with PMB, the center diameter of N, S-GQDs@PMB was 30.7 nm (Fig. 1B). Considering the different hydration states and the intensity-based weighting of the DLS data, the size difference between the TEM and DLS results was reasonable. The surface charge density of the nanoparticles was investigated by zeta potential analysis, and the results were shown in Fig. 1C. The zeta potential of N, S-GQDs was 6.77 mV, while the zeta potential of N, S-GQDs@PMB was 17.37 mV. This was due to the fact that PMB contained a large amount of positively charged free amino groups, and when PMB was modified on the surface of N, S-GQDs, the nanoparticle composites had a greater positive potential. To analyze the functional groups and elemental of N, S-GQDs@PMB, XPS and FTIR spectra were recorded. The full-scan XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 1D, and N, S-GQDs@PMB was mainly composed of C, O, N, and S. By further analysis, the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1E) revealed five characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.9 eV, 288.4 eV, and 289.5 eV. These peaks signified CC/CC, CN, CO, COOH, and OC-NH, respectively, indicating the sp2 orbital hybridization of carbon atoms and the abundant –COOH groups contained in N, S-GQDs. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S3A) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.5 eV, denoting C-NH and -NHx. The electronic binding energy position of the element O was 532.6 eV and 534.6 eV, corresponding to CO and H2O, respectively (Fig. S3B). The S 2p spectrum, displaying high resolution, revealed the existence of CSC and SOx units (Fig. S3C). Fig. 1F illustrated Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, showing characteristic absorption peaks of N, S-GQDs at about 1215 cm−1, 2553 cm−1, and 1701 cm−1. These peaks signified CN and/or CS bonds, stretching vibrations of SH bonds, and stretching vibrations of CO bonds in –COOH, respectively (Mondal, Mukherjee, Joardar, Roy, Chowdhury, & Sinha Babu, 2019). Due to numerous amino and amide bonds in PMB, characteristic absorption peaks were found at 1646 cm−1 and 1528 cm−1. These peaks corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CO bond and the stretching vibration of the NH bond in the amide bond (OC-NH), respectively (B. Yang, Chen, Wang, Deng, Feng, & Li, 2022). The stretching vibration of the CN bond in -C-NH2 was responsible for the absorption peak at 1077 cm−1. Notably, the infrared spectrum of N, S-GQDs@PMB covered the characteristic absorption peaks of both N, S-GQDs and PMB, further indicating that PMB had been successfully loaded into N, S-GQDs. In addition, the optical properties of N, S-GQDs@PMB were studied. The fluorescence spectrum of N, S-GQDs@PMB showed a redshift of 4 nm compared to N, S-GQDs (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1G) showed that the maximum absorption peak wavelength (λmax) of N, S-GQDs@PMB was blue-shifted. The shift of the maximum absorption peak was attributed to the changed in the refractive index of the N, S-GQDs@PMB surface. More importantly, N, S-GQDs@PMB exhibited the characteristic peak of PMB at 245–270 nm (M. Zhao et al., 2023). Given these phenomena, we concluded that PMB successfully binds to the surface of N, S-GQDs through amide bonds, and constructed energy donor N, S-GQDs@PMB with specific recognition function.

Carbon dots (CDs), as emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, are widely used in many fields because of their excellent characteristics, including good biocompatibility, stable fluorescence properties, broad spectral excitation response, low synthesis cost, and abundant functional groups (e.g., –NH2, –COOH, and –OH). By adjusting the reaction precursors and synthesis conditions, CDs with different excitation and emission spectra can be obtained (Huang, Song, Zhang, Chen, & Zhu, 2023; W.-B. Zhao et al., 2023). Inspired by that, we synthesized yellow luminescent carbon dots (Y-CDs) by a one-pot solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine as the precursor to match the blue fluorescence emission of N, S-GQDs mentioned above. Then we used it as the energy acceptor for the assembly of ratiometric fluorescent probes with significant color changes. XPS analysis determined the information on the surface elemental composition, chemical state, and molecular structure of Y-CDs, and the results showed that Y-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, and O (Fig. S5A-D). Among them, the electron binding energy positions of N elements were 400.3 eV, 399.4 eV, and 398.6 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N, 1°/2° amino N, and pyridinic N, respectively, which proved the presence of amino groups in the Y-CDs and provided binding sites for the coupling of carboxylated aptamers. The aptamer was modified on the surface of Y-CDs by amide reaction to obtain Y-CDs@Apt (Fig. 1A). XPS analysis of Y-CDs@Apt showed that its elemental composition contained C, N, O, and P (Fig. 1H, 1I and Fig. S6A-C). Through further analysis, the high-resolution spectrum of P 2p revealed the presence of PO4 3− (Fig. 1I). The TEM image (inset of Fig. 1J) showed that the average diameter of Y-CDs was about 2.1 nm. The hydrated particle size includes the nuclei of the nanoparticles and the swollen gel clusters. The hydrated particle size of Y-CDs was 8.72 nm, while the hydrated particle size of Y-CDs@Apt was 18.17 nm (Fig. 1J). The results of the zeta potential analysis were shown in Fig. 1K, where the zeta potential of Y-CDs was −4.01 mV, while the zeta potential of Y-CDs@Apt was −7.84 mV. Furthermore, the optical properties of Y-CDs@Apt were studied. As seen in Fig. 1L, the absorption intensity of Y-CDs@Apt at 260 nm was significantly increased compared with that of Y-CDs. Y-CDs had a maximum absorption peak at 417 nm, whereas Y-CDs@Apt had a red-shifted absorption peak here. As the surface polarity of the Y-CDs changed after modifying the aptamer, the fluorescence maximum emission wavelength of Y-CDs@Apt was also red-shifted from 562 nm to 566 nm (Fig. 1M). These results demonstrated the successful functionalization of Y-CDs by Apt.

At present, most of the biosensors constructed based on the FRET mechanism for the detection of pathogenic bacteria use quenching agents such as graphene oxide (GO) or gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as energy acceptors (Gupta et al., 2023; Neema, Tomy, & Cyriac, 2020; Rm, Maroli, J, Ponmalai, & K, 2020), and the detection signals are input in the form of single peaks, which are easily disturbed by external factors and cause false positive results. In addition, some ratiometric fluorescence strategies based on the FRET mechanism typically use bacteria as carriers. When the energy donor and energy acceptor simultaneously capture the target bacteria, the spatial distance between the donor and acceptor is shortened, and then FRET is “turned on” to achieve the detection of the target bacteria (Feng, Xu, Li, Chen, & Miao, 2022; Li, Xu, Feng, Zhang, & Miao, 2023). However, the non-specific binding of the donor–acceptor and the narrow response system leads to the problem of high background signal. Here, we pioneered the introduction of the Fe@MWCNTs complex as a “noise purifier” to realize the free switching of “adsorption-desorption” and effective separation of the receptor, which reduced the interference of background signals.

The “noise purifier” was synthesized by a one-step method. The defect sites on the oxidized surface of MWCNTs are distributed with abundant carboxyl groups, and after activating the carboxyl groups with EDC and NHS, MWCNTs-COO− can bind to the positively charged CIP-Fe2+ (CIP and H+ in acidic solution undergo replacement reaction to generate CIP-Fe2+), forming Fe@MWCNTs complex (MWCNTs-COOFe-CIP). In addition, excessive Fe2+ is oxidized by oxygen in the air to Fe3+, causing the solution to appear yellow, which can be used as a basis for determining whether the reaction is complete (Fig. 2 A). To obtain the best magnetic absorption effect, we optimized the pH of the MES buffer, the ratio of MWCNTs: CIP, and the incubation time, respectively. Based on the color reaction caused by the generation of trivalent iron ions, the optimal conditions were determined as the pH of MES buffer was 5.2, MWCNTs: CIP = 1:2, and the incubation time was 45 min (Fig. S7A-C). As shown in the inset of Fig. 2B, the prepared Fe@MWCNTs solution was a homogeneous and stable dispersion system in the centrifuge tube. Placing it on a magnetic frame, the Fe@MWCNTs were adsorbed to the wall of the centrifuge tube under the magnetic force (Fig. 2B), which demonstrated that the composite had good magnetism. The surface morphology of the material was characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2C. CIP attachment to the surface of MWCNTs could be observed in Fe@MWCNTs. The EDS elemental speciation revealed the inclusion of C, O, and Fe elements in the composite nanomaterials, indicating the successful combination of MWCNTs and CIP (Fig. 2D). The functional groups and elemental composition of the composites were investigated by XPS analysis. The results showed that Fe@MWCNTs mainly consisted of C, O, and Fe (Fig. 2E). By further analysis, the C 1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2F) exhibited four characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, 287.0 eV, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO, CO, and OC-O, respectively. The electron binding energy positions of O elements were 532.8 eV, 531.9 eV, and 531.1 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and Fe-O, respectively (Fig. 2G). Besides, we also observed four characteristic peaks located at 711.1 eV, 724.5 eV, 713.2 eV, and 726.4 eV in the Fe 2p spectrum, in accordance with the Fe (II) 2p3/2, Fe (II) 2p1/2, Fe (III) 2p3/2, and Fe (III) 2p1/2 states, respectively, indicating the presence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ (Fig. 2H). In addition, the zeta potential of MWCNTs was –23.01 mV, the zeta potential of CIP was 4.25 mV, while the zeta potential of Fe@MWCNTs was −2.38 mV, which was between –23.01 mV and 4.25 mV (Fig. 2I). Collectively, the above characterization results proved the success of the preparation of Fe@MWCNTs complexes.

A necessary condition for FRET is a certain degree of spectral overlap between the donor emission and acceptor absorption spectra. As Fig. 3 B shows, there was a clear spectral overlap between the emission spectra of N, S-GQDs@PMB and the absorption spectra of Y-CDs@Apt (the shaded portion with slash). Therefore, N, S-GQDs@PMB act as donors, while Y-CDs@Apt act as acceptors. When the target bacteria were present in the detection system, PMB and Apt exhibited broad-spectrum and highly specific binding on the surface of E. coli O157:H7, respectively, which shortened the distance between N, S-GQDs@PMB and Y-CDs@Apt (<10 nm) and met the occurrence conditions of FRET (G. Zhang et al., 2023; Zhang, Liu, Yang, Li, Tao, & Song, 2023). To achieve maximum energy transfer efficiency, an appropriate excitation wavelength should be chosen to ensure that N, S-GQDs@PMB obtain maximum fluorescence intensity while avoiding direct excitation of Y-CDs@Apt. As shown in Fig. S8, Fig. 3C, and D, the optimal excitation wavelength of N, S-GQDs@PMB was 355 nm, which corresponds to the fluorescence emission (422 nm) that was capable of driving Y-CDs@Apt to emit strong fluorescence. In contrast, under 355 nm excitation, the fluorescence emission intensity of Y-CDs@Apt was negligible. Therefore, selecting an excitation wavelength of 355 nm could effectively avoid spectral interference and construct a stable FRET reaction system. To further investigate the energy transfer effect, the emission spectra of N, S-GQDs@PMB, Y-CDs@Apt, and FRET systems (N, S-GQDs@PMB + Y-CDs@Apt + E. coli O157:H7) were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm, respectively. The excited state energy of N, S-GQDs can be transferred to Y-CDs in a non-radiative manner through intermolecular dipole–dipole interactions (W.-C. Yang, Li, Ni, & Liu, 2023). Specifically, the external light source excites the N, S-GQDs to jump from the ground state to the excited state (N, S-GQDs*), and the N, S-GQDs* absorb the energy and transfer some or all of the energy to the Y-CDs, thus the Y-CDs are excited to Y-CDs*. When the excited state Y-CDs* transitions jump back to the ground state, a large amount of fluorescence is generated at 566 nm. As seen in Fig. 3E, the maximum emission wavelength of N, S-GQDs@PMB was near 422 nm, and that of Y-CDs@Apt was near 566 nm. A significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N, S-GQDs@PMB and an increase in the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs@Apt were observed in the FRET system, which confirmed the energy transfer between them (Selvin, 2000).

It is well known that highly delocalized π bonds are formed between carbon atoms distributed in a hexagonal lattice in MWCNTs. The microscopic uniqueness of MWCNTs makes them adsorb large amounts of single-stranded DNA nucleic acid aptamers based on π-π stacking without the need for tedious and costly chemical coupling of the aptamers to the MWCNTs in advance (Jeong et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2020). To verify the feasibility of Fe@MWCNTs as the nanocarrier and magnetic separator, its “adsorption-desorption” ability was evaluated (Fig. 3A). As shown in Fig. 3F, adding Fe@MWCNTs to Y-CDs@Apt significantly reduced fluorescence intensity of supernatant after magnetic separation. This was due to the fact that nucleic acid aptamers were adsorbed onto the surface of Fe@MWCNTs under the π-π stacking force and were efficiently removed by magnetic separation. When E. coli O157:H7 was added to Y-CDs@Apt-Fe@MWCNTs and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min, the supernatant showed a significant recovery of fluorescence intensity after magnetic separation. This was due to the high affinity of the aptamer for the target bacteria. After the introduction of E. coli O157:H7 into the system, the aptamer underwent a 3D conformational change to produce specific binding sites for the target bacteria (Song, Li, & Xu, 2023). Y-CDs@Apt “desorbed” from the surface of Fe@MWCNTs and bound highly specifically to E. coli O157:H7, remaining in the supernatant after magnetic separation. The above results indicated that Fe@MWCNTs could achieve the free switching of “adsorption-desorption” and effective separation of the energy acceptors that were not bound to the target bacteria in the reaction system. Due to the non-specific binding of donors and acceptors or the narrow reaction space, the existing FRET sensors have the problem of high signal-to-noise ratio when applied to the detection of pathogenic bacteria. Fe@MWCNTs solved the above problem and improved the detection sensitivity and linear response range. To further validate the feasibility of the whole reaction system, fluorescence spectra of blank control and spiked samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 3G, in the blank control group, Y-CDs@Apt were adsorbed on the surface of Fe@MWCNTs due to π - π interactions and were removed by magnetic separation. After adding N, S-GQDs@PMB, it showed a high fluorescence intensity only at 422 nm. In contrast, when E. coli O157:H7 was present, Y-CDs@Apt was desorbed from Fe@MWCNTs and captured the target bacteria together with N, S-GQDs@PMB, the spatial distance between donor and acceptor was shortened. The energy was transferred from N, S-GQDs@PMB to Y-CDs@Apt, resulting in the attenuation of fluorescence intensity at 422 nm and enhancement of fluorescence intensity at 566 nm. The experimental results confirmed the feasibility of the designed platform for detecting E. coli O157:H7. Furthermore, the excessively low background signal of the negative results demonstrated the positive effect of the “noise purifier”.

FRET-based sensors have positive applications in the detection of hazardous substances. Unfortunately, when applied to detecting pathogenic bacteria, excessively high background signals lead to low sensitivity and poor response range. Inspired by the “noise purifier” mentioned above, we proposed a novel model that utilized a “noise purifier” by adsorbing acceptors in the reaction system that were not bound to the target bacteria to reduce the fluorescence interference, thus realizing an ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection mode and a smartphone-assisted two-color-varying colorimetric mode (Fig. 4 A). Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of the NP-RF biosensor and the RF biosensor without the “noise purifier” were evaluated. For different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7, the ratiometric fluorescence spectra of the NP-RF biosensor were shown in Fig. 4B. In the range of 101 to 107 cfu/mL, the detected signal (F566 nm/F422 nm) showed a linear dependence on the concentration of the target bacteria, with an equation of y = 0.1503x − 0.0475, R2 = 0.9967 (Fig. 4C), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 4.45 cfu/mL. Notably, the RF biosensor had a linear range of 102 to 107 cfu/mL (y = 0.1811x + 0.0382, R2 = 0.9949) (Fig. 4E and F), with an LOD of 4.74 × 101 cfu/mL.

It is well known that field detection is a coveted technology for pathogen control. With the development of smartphones, a new preferred detection strategy based mainly on RGB signal output has emerged. Taking RGB analysis based on smartphone-integrated devices as the second detection mode, Fig. 4D and G showed the detection results of the NP-on-site biosensor implanted with the “noise purifier” and the on-site biosensor without the “noise purifier”, respectively. The comparison showed that as the concentration of E. coli O157:H7 increased, the color of the NP-on-site biosensor gradually changed from blue to yellow. Compared with the light yellow to yellow of the on-site biosensor, its visual detection advantage was more obvious. Further, the color signals were read through the color recognition application (Color Picker APP) that came with the smartphone, and it was found that the obtained signal value R/B was dependent on the concentration of the target bacterial. The linear response range of the NP-on-site biosensor was 101 to 107 cfu/mL (y = 0.3345x + 0.1440, R2 = 0.9869), and the LOD was 8.76 cfu/mL. In comparison, the linear response range of the on-site biosensor was 102 to 107 cfu/mL (y = 0.3301x + 0.4431, R2 = 0.9842), with an LOD of 9.75 × 101 cfu/mL. These results indicated that the application of the “noise purifier” effectively reduced the interference of the background signal, expanded the response range of the target bacteria, and improved the detection sensitivity by an order of magnitude.

In food environments, where multiple pathogenic bacteria with similar antigenic sites are often present, the specificity of the detection platform is also an essential attribute in assessing the effectiveness of the assay. Eight non-E. coli strains and four non-O157:H7 E. coli strains were selected as interfering bacteria to evaluate the selectivity of the biosensor. As shown in Fig. 4H-J and Fig. S10, the F566 nm/F422 nm value (NP-RF biosensor) and R/B value (NP-on-site biosensor) of the E. coli O157:H7 group were larger than those of the other groups. In contrast, there was no significant difference between the signal output of these interfering bacteria at 106 cfu/mL and the bacterial-free blank control, indicating that the assay platform possessed high specificity for E. coli O157:H7.

Comparing the assay results with the traditional plate counting method, we found no significant differences (Fig. S11A). Notably, compared with the traditional culture method, the NP biosensor we developed has the advantages of a short detection period and easy operation, which can meet the requirements for rapid screening of E. coli O157:H7. In addition, we evaluated the precision, accuracy, reproducibility, and stability of the NP biosensor. As shown in Fig. S11B and C, the test was repeated 10 times by the developed sensor on the same sample with E. coli O157:H7 concentration of 106 cfu/mL. The relative standard deviations (R.S.D) of the NP-RF biosensor and the NP-on-site biosensor were 2.87 % and 3.19 %, and the average recoveries were 98.82 % and 104.38 %, respectively, indicating that the sensor has good precision and accuracy. Ten groups of assay systems prepared from the same batch and different batches were randomly selected, respectively, and the reproducibility of the NP biosensor was evaluated by analyzing the intragroup and intergroup test differences. As shown in Fig. S11D and E, the intragroup and intergroup R.S.D of the NP-RF biosensor were 3.01 % and 3.22 %, respectively, and the intragroup and intergroup R.S.D of the NP-on-site biosensor were 3.43 % and 3.84 %, respectively, which showed good reproducibility. The stability of the biosensor is a key factor in practical applications. As shown in Fig. S11F, the response signal stabilized after two weeks of storage at 4 °C, indicating that the stability of the NP biosensor is acceptable.

In Table S1, we have summarized the latest research advances in the rapid detection of E. coli O157:H7. Compared with those methods, our detection limits and linear ranges are superior. Most detection methods for E. coli are single-channel signal outputs, which are susceptible to the influence of the food substrate. Magnetic nanomaterials are usually used to eliminate the interference from food substrate. However, cumbersome and expensive chemical coupling is required between traditional magnetic beads and the recognition element, and their relatively large spatial volume interferes with the signal output of the nanomaterials (Yin et al., 2016). To make matters worse, although smartphone-assisted portable sensors avoid the use of large instruments, they mostly have inconspicuous color changes, resulting in low detection sensitivity. The limitations of the above assays are well overcome in this study: 1. The “noise purifier” combined with the ratiometric fluorescence strategy with built-in self-calibration function achieves higher detection sensitivity and wider dynamic linear range. 2. The smartphone-assisted two-color colorimetric sensing platform does not require expensive large-scale instruments and has the advantages of portability, real-time, and easy operation. In combination with the NP-on-site biosensor's two-color fluorescence response to E. coli O157:H7, highly sensitive field detection can be achieved. 3. Due to the large specific surface area and surface π-bonding, Fe@MWCNTs can be used as nanocarriers to adsorb a large number of Y-CDs@Apt based on non-covalent interactions, which omits the chemical coupling process and saves the detection cost. More excitingly, the reverse magnetic adsorption strategy in this study eliminates the effect of magnetic nanomaterials on the signal output of the sensor. 4. Fluorescent probes and recognition elements (PMB and Apt) are inexpensive, which further reduces the cost of detection.

To further validate the practicality of the biosensors, we used the on-site biosensor (Control-group, C-group) and the NP-on-site biosensor (Noise purifier-group, NP-group) to quantitatively detect E. coli O157: H7 in milk, beef, and drinking water, respectively. As shown in Table. 1 , we added three different concentrations (101, 103, and 105 cfu/mL) of E. coli O157:H7 to each sample. The recoveries of the on-site biosensor ranged from 86 % to 120 % with R.S.D of 4 % to 10 %. In contrast, the NP-on-site biosensor showed higher accuracy and repeatability with recoveries ranging from 91 % to 104 % and R.S.D of 3 % to 8 %. In addition, when the target bacterial concentration was low (101 cfu/mL), the detection results of the on-site biosensor were all negative (not detected), while the NP-on-site biosensor showed positive results. When used for actual sample detection, the NP-on-site biosensor has higher sensitivity, which further indicates that the “noise purifier” can greatly improve the detection performance. Therefore, the NP-on-site biosensor is a sensitive and reliable method for detecting pathogenic bacteria.

In summary, we developed and constructed a bicolor-ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, which was smartphone-assisted and relied on a “noise purifier”. The sensor utilized N, S-GQDs with high fluorescence emission efficiency as donors, and selected acceptor materials (Y-CDs) with excitation spectra that highly overlapped with the emission spectra of N, S-GQDs, which ensured the energy transfer efficiency. Further, the aptamers modified on the acceptor probes (Y-CDs@Apt) were adsorbed on the surfaces of MWCNTs by π-π stacking, which avoided the expensive and tedious chemical coupling and realized the flexible switching of “adsorption-desorption” for Y-CDs@Apt. The Fe@MWCNTs formed by the magnetization of MWCNTs had the ability of reverse magnetic attraction, which could be used as a “noise purifier” to solve the problem of low sensitivity of existing FRET sensors in pathogen detection due to high background signals. Compared with the RF biosensor, the NP-RF biosensor has a wider linear range (101–107 cfu/mL) and a higher detection sensitivity (LOD: 4.45 cfu/mL) than the RF biosensor. In addition, since the constructed bicolor-ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits concentration-dependent color changes, colorimetric analysis with a smartphone-integrated device enables portable on-site detection of E. coli O157:H7. As the concentration of the target bacteria increased, the color of the NP-on-site biosensor gradually changed from blue to yellow, which was more advantageous in visual detection compared with the light yellow to yellow of the on-site biosensor. The LOD of the NP-on-site biosensor was 8.76 cfu/mL, and the recoveries and R.S.D of the actual samples were 91–104 % and 3–8 %, respectively. The LOD of the on-site biosensor was 9.75 × 101 cfu/mL, and the recoveries and R.S.D of the actual samples were 86–120 % and 4–10 %, respectively. The detection performance of the NP-on-site biosensor was obviously better than that of the on-site biosensor, further confirming the positive role of the “noise purifier” in biosensors. More classically, the dual recognition strategy based on PMB (Gram-negative bacterial recognition element) and aptamers is unprecedented in pathogenic bacteria detection. By replacing the aptamer in combination with the efficient energy transfer system (N, S-GQDs/Y-CDs) based on the “noise purifier”, highly sensitive detection of other Gram-negative bacteria can be realized, which is promising for a wide range of applications in field detection.
